chapter_summary: Chapter 7 argues that the concept of 'general reading-comprehension skills' is a scientific delusion used to justify unorganized curricula, whereas actual literacy depends on domain-specific background knowledge. The author uses cognitive science, specifically the limits of short-term memory and the 'baseball study,' to demonstrate that reading is a process of active inference dependent on a shared communicative store rather than transferable abstract skills.

claims.text: The claim made by schools that they are teaching general 'reading-comprehension skills' is a delusion.
claims.source_passage: Current science explains why the claim made by our schools that they are teaching general “reading-comprehension skills” is a delusion.

claims.text: The notion of general reading-comprehension skills was established to defend a 'helter-skelter' curriculum often decided by individual teachers.
claims.source_passage: It’s a notion that was put in place to defend a helter-skelter curriculum that often had to be decided by the classroom teachers themselves.

claims.text: There are no general reading comprehension skills such as 'finding the main idea.'
claims.source_passage: Since there are no general reading comprehension skills like “finding the main idea,” once a person learns to sound out the words, the entire skill of reading depends on applying the right background knowledge.

claims.text: Once a person can decode words, the skill of reading depends entirely on applying relevant background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: once a person learns to sound out the words, the entire skill of reading depends on applying the right background knowledge.

claims.text: Background knowledge is the only mechanism that enables a person to disambiguate and amplify word meanings.
claims.source_passage: That alone enables a person to disambiguate and amplify the meanings of the written words.

claims.text: Successful communication requires writer and reader to share the same relevant background knowledge to infer implied meanings.
claims.source_passage: Hence the writer and the reader, speaker and listener, must share the same relevant background knowledge to infer (carry from) what had been implied in (folded into) the words.

claims.text: Knowledge of a content domain is a powerful determinant of the quality and amount of information a reader recalls.
claims.source_passage: It appeared to teachers therefore that knowledge of a content domain is a powerful determinant of the amount and quality of information recalled

claims.text: Content knowledge is powerful enough to allow poor readers to compensate for low reading ability.
claims.source_passage: powerful enough for poor readers to compensate for their generally low reading ability.

claims.text: The term 'generally low reading ability' is essentially a proxy for low national-ethnic background knowledge shared by a society's good readers.
claims.source_passage: But what is “generally low reading ability,” given adequate decoding? It simply means low national-ethnic background knowledge shared by the good readers in a society.

claims.text: Reading does not follow an orderly, bottom-up pattern of identifying words, then meanings, then sentences, then whole texts.
claims.source_passage: For one thing, new research refuted the old thinking that reading follows an orderly pattern. We don’t first identify words, then word meanings, then combine word meanings to get the meanings of sentences, and finally combine sentence meanings to get the meaning of a whole text.

claims.text: The reader acts as a supplier of essential cultural information that is not explicitly written in the text.
claims.source_passage: The new picture that has emerged from language research is more complicated and more useful. It brings to the fore the highly active mind of the reader, who is now discovered to be not only a decoder of what is written down, but also a supplier of much essential cultural information that is not written down.

claims.text: Explicit written meanings are only a small fraction of the total meaning; the larger part resides in the reader's knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The explicit meanings of a piece of writing are the tip of an iceberg of meaning; the larger part lies below the surface of the text and is composed of the reader’s own relevant knowledge about the subject

claims.text: Human short-term memory capacity is severely limited to approximately four to seven separate items.
claims.source_passage: The mind cannot reliably hold more than about four to seven separate items in short term memory, whether numbers, letters, points on the body, degrees of loudness, degrees of warmth, and so on.

claims.text: The capacity limit of short-term memory is hardwired into the human system and cannot be directly overcome.
claims.source_passage: Memory experts have worked out indirect tricks to circumvent this universal limitation, but they can never directly overcome it, because the constraint is hardwired into our systems.

claims.text: Failure to perform a task reliably occurs whenever the task consistently forces the individual to exceed the capacity of short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: Whenever we try to perform a task that consistently forces us to exceed the capacity of short-term memory, we fail to perform it reliably.

claims.text: 'Chunking' is a psychological device that allows people to group discrete items together to resist short-term memory limits.
claims.source_passage: One device that memory experts have developed to resist this built-in limitation is “chunking.” We can remember telephone numbers and other series of discrete items if we chunk the disparate items into a smaller number of groupings.

claims.text: Human beings cannot reliably remember more than four to seven discrete items that have been presented once without rehearsal.
claims.source_passage: ly remember more than four to seven discrete items that have been presented once without rehearsal. That is a limit on memory experts, as well as on ordinary people.

claims.text: Chunking allows the mind to treat multiple discrete items as a single grouping, thereby resisting the built-in limitations of short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: One device that memory experts have developed to resist this built-in limitation is “chunking.” We can remember telephone numbers and other series of discrete items if we chunk the disparate items into a smaller number of groupings.

claims.text: Short-term memory functions as a temporary structure-building vestibule for incoming linguistic items.
claims.source_passage: Short-term memory is the mind’s vestibule where incoming items enjoy a brief equality lasting just long enough for the mind to give them a structure.

claims.text: Short-term memory enables the conversion of temporal language flow into stable, nontemporal structures.
claims.source_passage: Short-term memory holds in momentary suspension items that have come in one after another, thus enabling us to convert them into a stable structure. Amidst the temporal flow of language, short-term memory allows us to form a few words into nontemporal structures.

claims.text: The universal linguistic requirement to divide sentences into brief clauses is caused by the physical limitations of short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: The limitation imposed by short-term memory accounts for the fact that all languages must form brief bursts of words into clauses. Every known language divides its sentences into semi-complete clausal units that are small enough to be structured within the limitations of short-term memory.

claims.text: Language is transferred to long-term memory as a shorthand recoding of the 'gist' rather than a literal recollection of specific words.
claims.source_passage: Bever says that language is transferred from short-term memory into long-term memory not as a literal recollection of words, but as a shorthand recoding of their gist, which normally erases from memory many of the individual words.

claims.text: Human long-term memory for meaning (gist) is reliable, while memory for literal wording is poor.
claims.source_passage: On the basis of this account, we could predict that our memories for the literal words that make up sentences should be poor, whereas our long-term memories for their gist should be quite reliable. That is indeed the case.

claims.text: Evolution favors the retention of meanings over the literal surface forms of experience to prevent cognitive overload.
claims.source_passage: From an evolutionary standpoint it is much more desirable that we forget the literal words we encounter and remember their meanings than that we have an absolute memory for both words and meanings. If we remembered all the surface details of our experiences... the hard disks in our minds would quickly fill to capacity.

claims.text: The surface forms of sentences are typically lost from memory within a few seconds.
claims.source_passage: In fact, experimenters have found that surface forms of sentences are lost to memory within a few seconds.

claims.text: The original form of a sentence is rapidly lost to memory, whereas an accurate memory for its meaning is retained.
claims.source_passage: Sachs inferred from these results, and later experimentation has amply confirmed her inference, that the original form of a sentence is rapidly lost to memory, whereas an accurate memory for its meaning is retained.

claims.text: Inferences regarding sentence meaning are made at the time a sentence is understood, not later during recognition or recall.
claims.source_passage: Most remarkable of all, it was discovered about a decade later that inferences regarding open versus not closed and short versus not tall are made at the time a sentence is understood, not later in recognition or recall.

claims.text: Accurate memory of surface form normally disappears in a few seconds, while accurate memory for gist is quite persistent.
claims.source_passage: The two allied phenomena are now firmly established–it is known that accurate memory of surface form normally disappears in a few seconds, while accurate memory for gist is quite persistent.

claims.text: Initial understanding of a text depends on applying relevant background knowledge that is not provided in the text itself.
claims.source_passage: In a series of experiments, they found that our initial understanding of a text depends on our applying relevant background knowledge that is not given in the text itself.

claims.text: Human beings go beyond a text's literal meanings to supply important implications that are not explicitly stated by the words of the text.
claims.source_passage: Under this second theory, we always go beyond a text’s literal meanings to supply important implications that were not explicitly stated by the words of the text.

claims.text: The constructive hypothesis of language understanding is more accurate than the theory that we simply store literal meanings of clauses and sentences.
claims.source_passage: The constructive hypothesis, in other words, proved to be correct.

claims.text: Memory loss for exact wording occurs very rapidly, potentially within only sixteen intervening syllables.
claims.source_passage: Eric Wanner showed that listeners had already lost the exact wording of a sentence when only sixteen syllables intervened between the sentence and the test.

claims.text: The human mind stores the full model of a text's constructed meaning in long-term memory rather than its literal wording.
claims.source_passage: We then store the fuller model of the text’s meaning in long-term memory.

claims.text: Inferences based on prior knowledge are an intrinsic part of verbal meaning from the moment of initial understanding.
claims.source_passage: The answer given by this, and other experiments is “Yes, that is the nature of verbal meaning-inferences based on prior knowledge are part of meaning from the very beginning.”

claims.text: There are mutual influences between the mental models in a reader's mind based on prior knowledge and the actual words of the text.
claims.source_passage: From this finding we can predict that there will be mutual influences between the mental models we have in our minds on the basis of prior knowledge along with the words of the text as we read them.

claims.text: An integrated mental model is essential for the understanding and remembering of discourse.
claims.source_passage: Apparently, such an integrated model is essential to understanding and remembering discourse.

claims.text: People who lack cultural knowledge are unable to make sense of whole texts because they cannot interpret essential referential clues, even if they can read individual sentences.
claims.source_passage: Informationally deprived people constantly run across passages that look like the Bransford one, because the texts contain important referential clues they can’t understand. Although they can read the individual sentences, they can’t make sense out of the whole.

claims.text: Differences in reading ability between five-year-olds and eight-year-olds are caused primarily by the older children’s possessing more knowledge, rather than differences in memory capacity, reasoning abilities, or eye movement control.
claims.source_passage: T. Trabasso and his colleagues discovered that differences in reading ability between five-year-olds and eight-year-olds are caused primarily by the older children’s possessing more knowledge, not by differences in their memory capacities, reasoning abilities, or control of eye movements.

claims.text: Differences in reading ability are not caused by differences in memory capacities, reasoning abilities, or eye movement control.
claims.source_passage: not by differences in their memory capacities, reasoning abilities, or control of eye movements.

claims.text: Among children with identical reading and IQ scores, those with greater knowledge relevant to a specific text demonstrate superior reading skills on that text.
claims.source_passage: Similar work by P. D. Pearson and his associates has shown that, among seven-year-olds who score the same on reading and IQ tests, those who have greater knowledge relevant to the text at hand show superior reading skills.

claims.text: Reading performance can be significantly improved by background knowledge even when the text contains no difficult words or special concepts.
claims.source_passage: Those who knew a little about them were much better readers of a story involving spiders than were the other children, even though the story contained no special concepts or difficult words.

claims.text: The educational establishment has failed to acknowledge or implement the practical implications of research showing knowledge is the primary driver of reading comprehension.
claims.source_passage: One might be tempted to say that these results are predictable and obvious. Nevertheless, educators have not acknowledged or acted on the implications of such results.

claims.text: Cognitive experience is inherently partial and fragmented, requiring past knowledge to interpret momentary sensory data into functional wholes.
claims.source_passage: All continuing experience is partial and fragmented like my view of those trees. Our cognitive life takes place through a small window of attention that is framed by short-term memory. We use past knowledge to interpret this window of experience, to place its momentary fragments within larger wholes that give them a function and a place.

claims.text: Linguistic and visual perception remains meaningless unless fragments are placed within larger, not presently visible structures based on past knowledge.
claims.source_passage: In our minds these transitory fragments can acquire meaning only by being placed within larger, not presently visible wholes that are based on past knowledge.

claims.text: In the absence of specific constraints, humans naturally interpret experiences through 'middle categories' that are neither too specific nor too general.
claims.source_passage: The answer is that, if no other constraints are at work, people tend to interpret their experiences through middle categories, that is, through classifications that are neither specific, like oak leaves, nor general, like flora.

claims.text: Middle-level categories are the most efficient instruments for perceiving the world and taking action within it.
claims.source_passage: We normally interpret experience through the categories that have been most useful to us in the past. We try them out initially as the most efficient instruments for perceiving the world and acting in it.

claims.text: Middle-level categories serve as the fundamental framework or 'basic furniture' of the human conceptual world.
claims.source_passage: In a number of papers she has argued that middle-level categories are the basic furniture of our conceptual world.

claims.text: People within a culture exhibit high levels of consistency regarding which specific examples are 'typical' members of a category.
claims.source_passage: In one experiment Rosch asked her subjects to arrange some specific names under the categories fruit, furniture, bird, and clothing, ranking the names from the most to the least typical. The results were quite consistent from person to person.

claims.text: Language users automatically assume 'typical' members of a category when encountering general class names in isolation.
claims.source_passage: If people in our culture tend to agree that a robin is more typical than a pheasant in the bird category, it is likely that, other things being equal, they will think of a robin-sort-of-creature when they hear the word bird.

claims.text: Sentences using a general class name are perceived as making sense only if the specific member substituted for that class name matches the typical behavior of the class prototype.
claims.source_passage: In isolated sentences, when an untypical member of a class is substituted for the class name, sentences that previously sounded reasonable often fall into absurdity.

claims.text: Shared ethnicity and literacy are defined by agreement on typical examples of categories.
claims.source_passage: People with the same ethnicity and literacy agree about typical examples of a class and the isolated sentences they make with class names work best with typical examples.

claims.text: Typical exemplars or prototypes of category words constitute the 'usual furniture of our minds' and are used daily in perception and communication.
claims.source_passage: This finding suggests that we hold in our minds typical exemplars or prototypes of the category words we use and that these prototypes constitute the usual furniture of our minds.

claims.text: Prototypes of categories are processed significantly faster by the brain than atypical examples.
claims.source_passage: Rosch and others have shown that such prototypes are processed much faster than atypical examples in verifying the truth or falsity of isolated sentences.

claims.text: The prototype for the category 'bird' in American culture is a vaguely robin-like creature.
claims.source_passage: For a bird in American culture, the prototype is a vaguely robin-like creature.

claims.text: Knowledge of prototypes is essential for understanding how people of a specific ethnicity apply past knowledge to comprehend speech.
claims.source_passage: Knowing about prototypes is essential for understanding how the people within a given ethnicity apply past knowledge to the comprehension of speech.

claims.text: Human beings make sense of present experiences by assimilating them to prototypes formed from past experiences.
claims.source_passage: We are able to make our present experiences take on meaning by assimilating them to prototypes formed from our past experiences.

claims.text: Mental prototypes are better described as 'schemata'—abstract mental entities—than as concrete images.
claims.source_passage: Schema and its plural schemata correctly suggest somewhat abstract mental entities rather than concrete images.

claims.text: Category prototypes (schemata) vary significantly between different cultures or ethnicities.
claims.source_passage: If Rosch had tested her subjects in Australia, her results would probably have been different, and she would have found the typical bird schema to be unlike a robin in many respects.

claims.text: The initial schemata applied to a word are sensitive to the specific context of the sentence.
claims.source_passage: They have found that the initial schemata under which we create and interpret isolated sentences are not necessarily the initial schemata we apply in other contexts.

claims.text: The human mind possesses a remarkable ability to adjust initial conceptions unconsciously based on the situation.
claims.source_passage: The research with the word list just referred to has shown that our minds have a remarkable ability to change initial conception according to the situations in which we find ourselves.

claims.text: Readers automatically 'hypothecate' or infer specific details about objects (like the type of ball) based on the subject performing the action.
claims.source_passage: A different sort of ball is implied by each sentence. The punter is kicking a football, and the golfer a golf ball.

claims.text: Human minds have a remarkable ability to change initial conceptions according to the specific situations in which we find ourselves.
claims.source_passage: The research with the word list just referred to has shown that our minds have a remarkable ability to change initial conception according to the situations in which we find ourselves.

claims.text: Adjustments to initial mental conceptions are largely unconscious.
claims.source_passage: Such adjustments are largely unconscious, but one can perceive the pattern of the quick adjustments we make even with isolated sentences

claims.text: In reading, individuals adjust their initial schemata to the specific requirements of the text.
claims.source_passage: In reading, for example, we adjust our initial schemata to the specific text. In a story about a Thanksgiving feast, our initial schema for bird is turkey, not robin.

claims.text: Reading involves a two-way traffic between ethnic schemata and the words on the page.
claims.source_passage: Two-way traffic takes place between our ethnic schemata and the words we read. We apply past schemata to make sense of the incoming words, but these words and other contextual clues affect our initial choices of schemata and our continuing adjustment of them.

claims.text: Reading is an active process of selecting and adjusting appropriate schemata rather than a passive reception of meaning.
claims.source_passage: the reader is not just passively receiving meaning but is actively selecting the most appropriate schemata for making sense of the incoming words. Then the reader actively adjusts those schemata to the incoming words until a good fit is achieved.

claims.text: Reading process efficiency depends on having quick access to appropriate schemata.
claims.source_passage: This process can work efficiently only if the reader has quick access to appropriate schemata.

claims.text: When appropriate schemata are not quickly available, the limits of short-term memory are reached and the reading process must be restarted.
claims.source_passage: When the appropriate schemata are not quickly available, and the reader is forced to do a lot of pondering to construct them at the time of reading, the limits of short-term memory are quickly reached, and the process has to be painfully restarted.

claims.text: Educators can make intelligent practical advances in teaching literacy without knowing the fine psychological details of schema processing.
claims.source_passage: But we do not have to know the fine detail of the process to make intelligent practical advances in the teaching of literacy.

claims.text: Schemata perform two essential functions for literacy: storing knowledge in retrievable form and organizing it for efficient application.
claims.source_passage: We know that schemata perform two essential functions that are relevant to literacy. The first is storing knowledge in retrievable form; the second is organizing knowledge in more and more efficient ways, so that it can be applied rapidly and efficiently.

claims.text: Without appropriate background knowledge, individuals cannot adequately understand written or spoken language.
claims.source_passage: Without appropriate background knowledge, people cannot adequately understand written or spoken language.

claims.text: Slowness of reading beyond a certain point makes assimilation of complex meaning impossible.
claims.source_passage: Slowness of reading beyond a certain point makes assimilation of complex meaning impossible. The limits of short-term memory do not allow the integration of “un-chunked” material, and so crucial parts of meaning are lost to memory, while other parts are being painstakingly worked out.

claims.text: Schemata serve as a mental shorthand that helps readers stay within the limits of short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: The evidence has shown that schemata are useful as a kind of mental shorthand that helps us work within the limits of short-term memory.

claims.text: Speed of comprehension is functionally equivalent to quality of comprehension because slow processing leads to cognitive circuit overload.
claims.source_passage: In this process, speed of comprehension is equivalent to quality of comprehension, because without the speed and the shorthand provided by well-organized schemata, our circuits get overloaded.

claims.text: A well-developed schema allows a person to deploy a complex complex of subterranean elements as though they were a single item.
claims.source_passage: a well-developed schema not only helps us make sense of incoming data but also helps us to manipulate and connect information rapidly.

claims.text: Human memory limits prevent the successful manipulation of information if the reader lacks the shorthand provided by schemata.
claims.source_passage: We can’t perform the necessary manipulations before we run out of memory, and we continually have to start over again.

claims.text: In a schema, the knowledge most frequently required for communication is the most directly available at the surface.
claims.source_passage: Their theory is that the knowledge most often needed is also the most directly available and is, so to speak, right at the surface of the schema.

claims.text: The top portion of a schema is the most important part to know for the purpose of rapid deployment in reading.
claims.source_passage: Collins and Quillian’s observations suggest that the top portion of a schema is the important part to know.

claims.text: Functional communication relies on the distinctive system of traits in schemata that differentiate concepts rather than secondary biological or physical facts.
claims.source_passage: What is functional in reading, writing, and conversing is the distinctive system of traits in the schemata.

claims.text: Readers must know the primary traits commonly associated with a term in their culture to deploy associations rapidly during reading.
claims.source_passage: We need to know the primary traits commonly associated with canary in our culture to deploy the associations rapidly when we encounter the word canary in reading.

claims.text: Reading and conversing require calling up primary associations in milliseconds; there is no luxury to figure them out one at a time.
claims.source_passage: They must be called up with lightning speed in the course of reading and conversing. We do not have the luxury of figuring out such associations one at a time.

claims.text: Successful communication depends upon shared associations.
claims.source_passage: Successful communication depends upon shared associations.

claims.text: Adult-level discourse never retreats to the rudiments of knowledge; it assumes them to maintain brevity and clarity.
claims.source_passage: No adult level discourse retreats to the rudiments of knowledge. If assumptions about rudiments could not be made, ordinary discourse would be so lengthy and intricate as to obscure its own point.

claims.text: Literacy requires both intensive knowledge of relationships and extensive knowledge of specifics.
claims.source_passage: But literacy requires us to have both intensive knowledge of relationships and extensive knowledge of specifics.

claims.text: Dictionaries and encyclopedias are impractical tools for comprehension if a reader constantly lacks crucial background information.
claims.source_passage: When readers constantly lack crucial information, dictionaries and encyclopedias become quite impractical tools.

claims.text: A consistent lack of necessary information can make the reading process so laborious and uncommunicative that it fails to convey meaning.
claims.source_passage: A consistent lack of necessary information can make the reading process so laborious and uncommunicative that it fails to convey meaning.

claims.text: The ability of humans to exercise a skill depends on their possession of specific schemata that are sufficiently numerous and detailed to handle specific tasks.
claims.source_passage: AI research demonstrates that the ability of humans to exercise a skill depends on their possession of specific schemata that are sufficiently numerous and detailed to handle the many varieties of the tasks they are called on to perform.

claims.text: It is more accurate to use the plural 'reading skills' because proficiency is tied to domain-specific knowledge rather than a general faculty.
claims.source_passage: It is more accurate to speak of “reading skills” than of “reading skill.”

claims.text: A skill is not a unified system of intellectual muscles that can be developed by calisthenics into a vigorous all-purpose ability.
claims.source_passage: a skill is not a unified system of intellectual muscles that can be developed by calisthenics into a vigorous all-purpose ability.

claims.text: Schools cannot effectively teach reading, writing, and critical thinking as all-purpose general skills applicable to novel problems.
claims.source_passage: The discoveries that he and his coworkers made should induce a deep skepticism toward the belief that our schools can teach reading, writing, and critical thinking as all-purpose general skills applicable to novel problems.

claims.text: All cognitive skills depend on procedural and substantive schemata that are highly specific to the task at hand.
claims.source_passage: All cognitive skills depend on procedural and substantive schemata that are highly specific to the task at hand.

claims.text: Skill follows once the relevant knowledge has been acquired.
claims.source_passage: Once the relevant knowledge has been acquired, the skill follows.

claims.text: General programs contrived to teach general skills are ineffective.
claims.source_passage: General programs contrived to teach general skills are ineffective.

claims.text: Experts perform better than novices not because of better intellectual machinery, but because they have more relevant and quickly available information.
claims.source_passage: AI research shows that experts perform better than novices, not because they have more powerful and better-oiled intellectual machinery, but because they have more relevant and quickly available information.

claims.text: The primary distinction between good and poor readers is the possession of diverse, task-specific information.
claims.source_passage: What distinguishes good readers from poor ones is simply the possession of a lot of diverse, task-specific information.

claims.text: When the configuration of a task is significantly changed, past skills are not transferred to the new problem.
claims.source_passage: When the configuration of a task is significantly changed, past skills are not transferred to the new problem.

claims.text: Skill is not transferred beyond similar or analogous circumstances.
claims.source_passage: But beyond similar or analogous circumstances, skill is not transferred.

claims.text: The convergence of accounts from both artificial intelligence and empirical psychology increases confidence in the correctness of their models of expertise.
claims.source_passage: The convergence of the accounts from both AI and empirical psychology increases one’s confidence in their correctness.

claims.text: Artificial intelligence chess programs behave like skilled players because they imitate the knowledge-based procedures of human experts.
claims.source_passage: An up-to-date chess program behaves like a skilled player... because in some ways, though not in all, it imitates the knowledge-based procedures of experts.

claims.text: Expert performance in complex domains depends on the quick deployment of mental schemata.
claims.source_passage: Both approaches show that expert performance depends on the quick deployment of schemata.

claims.text: Expert chess players do not possess better general memory or general skills than novices.
claims.source_passage: In his second experiment de Groot proved that expert chess players have no better memories or general skills than novices. Like everyone else, they can remember only five or six pieces from a random pattern.

claims.text: Positional schemata allow experts to 'chunk' information, reducing the burden on short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: Because they already possess positional schemata, experts would need to remember only four or five chunked items well within the range of short-term memory.

claims.text: Skill in reading is structurally similar to skill in chess because both require the rapid deployment of pre-acquired schemata.
claims.source_passage: Skill in reading is like skill in chess in many respects. Good reading, like good chess, requires the rapid deployment of schemata that have already been acquired and do not have to be worked out on the spot.

claims.text: Good readers avoid short-term memory overload by ignoring small-scale textual features in favor of overall structure.
claims.source_passage: Good readers... quickly recognize typical patterns, and, since they can ignore many small-scale features of the text, they have space in short-term memory to take in an overall structure of meaning.

claims.text: Unskilled readers experience memory overload because they lack a store of relevant schemata and must process small-scale meaning relationships individually.
claims.source_passage: By contrast, unskilled readers lack this large store of relevant schemata and must therefore work out many small-scale meaning relationships while they are reading. These demanding tasks quickly overload their short-term memories.

claims.text: A number in the range of 50,000 marks the approximate upper limit of schemata items to which a human can have rapid, immediate access.
claims.source_passage: Apparently, a number in the range of 50,000 marks the upper limit of items to which we can have rapid access in any domain of activity.

claims.text: The set of 50,000 rapidly accessible schemata serves as a mental index for a much larger volume of knowledge stored in long-term memory.
claims.source_passage: A basic vocabulary of 50,000 schemata serves merely as a quickly accessible index to a much larger volume of knowledge.

claims.text: The most effective cognitive system for tasks like chess or reading is to maintain indexed schemata at a medium level of generality.
claims.source_passage: the most effective system for the mind, whether in chess or in reading, is to keep most of its indexed schemata at a medium level of generality.

claims.text: The human mind can store and relate roughly 50,000 schemata in long-term memory.
claims.source_passage: Any of the 50,000 schemata can be related to others, and the further relationships can be stored in long-term memory.

claims.text: A single schema can serve as a mental shorthand for enormous complexes of associated schemata.
claims.source_passage: A useful illustration of the way one schema can serve as a mental shorthand for enormous complexes of associated schemata is the field of mathematics

claims.text: Expertise in reading is structurally different from expertise in chess because reading requires social coordination of schemata.
claims.source_passage: But although parallels between skill in reading and skill in chess are informative, the two kinds of expertise are structurally different in one respect. Chess players do not have to communicate their stored patterns to anyone else and can organize their schemata in whatever way they like.

claims.text: Effective verbal communication requires that the patterns of association in one person's verbal schemata must approximate those of another.
claims.source_passage: The patterns of association in the verbal schemata of one person must approximate those of another, otherwise we could not use surface meanings to represent larger systems of subsurface associations.

claims.text: Words, idioms, and grammatical systems represent shared systems of association common to an ethnicity.
claims.source_passage: Words, idioms, and grammatical systems represent shared systems of association102 – common to an ethnicity.

claims.text: Literate adults perform communication tasks involving unfamiliar shapes with virtually no errors.
claims.source_passage: To establish a standard of performance, we run the experiment with pairs of literate adults, who find the task almost ridiculously easy. They make virtually no errors on their first attempt.

claims.text: Young children (ages 4-5) fail at communication tasks because their messages depend on associations not widely shared in the speech community.
claims.source_passage: Not one pair of children completes a trial correctly. Their messages are short and cryptic rather than full and elaborated like those of adults. Moreover, the messages depend upon associations that are not widely shared in the speech community.

claims.text: Even ninth-grade students fail to attain the perfect accuracy displayed by adults on initial trials of complex communication tasks.
claims.source_passage: We were again surprised at the generally low level of performance of ninth graders, although they showed dramatic improvement in successive trials, still did not attain the virtually perfect accuracy that adults displayed from the very first trial.

claims.text: Children's poor performance in communication is due to a lack of shared information and social insight rather than insufficient cognitive development.
claims.source_passage: Krauss and Glucksberg inferred from this that the older children performed poorly not because of their insufficient cognitive development, but because of their lack of information and insight into what other people might know.

claims.text: A lack of information regarding shared schemata strains a child's cognitive capacity during communication.
claims.source_passage: Their lack of information strained their cognitive capacities when they tried to carry out the task. They had not fully acquired American ethnicity.

claims.text: When communication demands are heavy, children are unable to bring into play the social communication skills they actually possess.
claims.source_passage: Finally, when the demands of the task become heavy enough, children may not have the opportunity to bring into play the social communication skills they possess.

claims.text: The communicative performance of young children in specific experimental tasks is analogous to the performance of poor readers.
claims.source_passage: The performance of the young children in this experiment is like the performance of poor readers.

claims.text: A lack of shared information strains cognitive capacities when individuals attempt to carry out communicative tasks.
claims.source_passage: Their lack of information strained their cognitive capacities when they tried to carry out the task.

claims.text: Children who lack the necessary communicative store of information have not fully acquired 'American ethnicity.'
claims.source_passage: They had not fully acquired American ethnicity.

claims.text: Children communicate successfully and engage in social, nonegocentric speech when the demands of the task are relatively light.
claims.source_passage: When the demands of the task are relatively light, children do engage in social, nonegocentric speech, and they communicate rather successfully.

claims.text: As communicative demands become heavier, children employ social communication strategies less effectively than adults.
claims.source_passage: As the demands become heavier, children may still attempt to employ social communication strategies, but they do so less effectively than adults.

claims.text: Heavy task demands can prevent children from bringing their existing social communication skills into play.
claims.source_passage: Finally, when the demands of the task become heavy enough, children may not have the opportunity to bring into play the social communication skills they possess.

claims.text: Both poor readers and young children in communicative tasks are ineffective due to a lack of information and resulting cognitive overload.
claims.source_passage: Both are ineffective because of lack of information and cognitive overload.

claims.text: Effective reading requires the reader to monitor whether their personal schematic associations are shared by the wider speech community.
claims.source_passage: In effective reading, one must not only call up one’s own schematic associations, but also monitor whether they are appropriate ones shared by the wider speech community.

claims.text: Literate adults have internalized shared schemata to the point that they function as second nature.
claims.source_passage: Literate adults have internalized these shared schemata and have made them second nature.

claims.text: Literate adults possess a surer instinct for identifying what information will or will not be shared by others in the wider culture.
claims.source_passage: As the Krauss-Glucksberg experiments indicate, literate adults have a surer instinct for what will and will not be shared by others in the wider culture.

claims.text: Semi-literates and young children lack readily accessible information regarding what other members of the speech community can be expected to know.
claims.source_passage: Thus, besides their lack of substantive information, young children and semi-literates also lack something equally important: readily accessible information about what is shared by others.

claims.text: Ethnic knowledge—or the communicative store—is not strictly a function of biological age.
claims.source_passage: Such ethnic knowledge is not entirely a function of age. Some children acquire it rather early, and illiterate adults never acquire it fully.

claims.text: Under current circumstances, communicative task performance is strongly correlated with social class.
claims.source_passage: Experiments reported by Basil Bernstein suggest that the performances of children... have, alas, under present circumstances, a strong correlation with social class.

claims.text: The primary variable in determining communicative success in Bernstein's experiments was the child's familiarity with talking to literate strangers.
claims.source_passage: In Bernstein’s experiments, the most important variable was whether the children were used to talking with literate strangers and had thereby gained wide knowledge and a sense of the wider speech community.

claims.text: Literacy is fundamentally a function of a national culture rather than a local culture.
claims.source_passage: Bernstein’s experiments reconfirmed that literacy is a function of a national rather than a local culture.

claims.text: The writer and the reader, or speaker and listener, must share the same relevant background knowledge to infer what has been implied or 'folded into' the words.
claims.source_passage: Hence the writer and the reader, speaker and listener, must share the same relevant background knowledge to infer (carry from) what had been implied in (folded into) the words.

claims.text: Memory experts utilize indirect tricks to circumvent short-term memory limitations, but they cannot directly overcome these constraints because they are hardwired into the human system.
claims.source_passage: Memory experts have worked out indirect tricks to circumvent this universal limitation, but they can never directly overcome it, because the constraint is hardwired into our systems.

claims.text: All languages divide sentences into brief bursts of words or semi-complete clausal units to accommodate the physical limitations of short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: The limitation imposed by short-term memory accounts for the fact that all languages must form brief bursts of words into clauses.

claims.text: The evolution of the human mind favors forgetting literal words to prioritize the retention of meanings, preventing the brain's 'hard disks' from reaching capacity.
claims.source_passage: From an evolutionary standpoint it is much more desirable that we forget the literal words we encounter and remember their meanings than that we have an absolute memory for both words and meanings. If we remembered all the surface details... the hard disks in our minds would quickly fill to capacity.

claims.text: Human beings tend to interpret experience through middle categories—classifications that are neither too specific nor too general—because they are the most efficient for action.
claims.source_passage: The answer is that, if no other constraints are at work, people tend to interpret their experiences through middle categories, that is, through classifications that are neither specific, like oak leaves, nor general, like flora. We normally interpret experience through the categories that have been most useful to us in the past.

claims.text: Children naturally learn 'basic-level' terms such as 'tree' or 'dog' before they acquire more specific terms like 'oak' or more general terms like 'animal'.
claims.source_passage: Research has shown that middle-level categories are the ones children learn first in acquiring language; they learn tree before oak, and they learn dog before animal.

claims.text: Prototypes are processed significantly faster than atypical examples when humans verify the truth or falsity of isolated sentences.
claims.source_passage: Rosch and others have shown that such prototypes are processed much faster than atypical examples in verifying the truth or falsity of isolated sentences.

claims.text: To improve the performance of semiliterate people, educators must apply the principles of schemata rather than focusing on general skills.
claims.source_passage: We need to know how to put the principles of schemata to work to improve the performance of semiliterate people.

claims.text: Successful communication relies on shared 'primary traits' within schemata—such as a canary's yellowness or ability to sing—rather than secondary biological facts.
claims.source_passage: What is functional in reading, writing, and conversing is the distinctive system of traits in the schemata. We use the traits that differentiate canaries from other birds... We need to know the primary traits commonly associated with canary in our culture to deploy the associations rapidly.

claims.text: Expert performance in any field is not the result of a 'dormative power' or general skill, but the possession of vast amounts of task-specific, quickly available information.
claims.source_passage: Dr. Herbert A. Simon... remarked that saying an expert performance is caused by a 'skill' is like Moliere’s doctor saying that the sleep-inducing properties of opium are caused by its 'dormative power.' ... What distinguishes good readers from poor ones is simply the possession of a lot of diverse, task-specific information.

claims.text: The performance of older children on communication tasks is hindered by their lack of insight into what information they share with strangers in their speech community.
claims.source_passage: Krauss and Glucksberg inferred from this that the older children performed poorly not because of their insufficient cognitive development, but because of their lack of information and insight into what other people might know.

evidence.description: An experiment showing that children with high baseball knowledge but low general reading ability outperformed 'good readers' with low baseball knowledge when reading about a baseball game.
evidence.source_reference: Recht, Donna R.; Leslie, Lauren, 'Effect of Prior Knowledge on Good and Poor Readers’ Memory of Text,' Journal of Educational Psychology (1988)

evidence.description: A national report's foreword stating that decades of research have provided unparalleled understanding of language comprehension processes.
evidence.source_reference: R. Glaser, 'Foreword,' in Becoming a Nation of Readers (1985)

evidence.description: Path-breaking psychological research establishing the universal constraint of short-term memory capacity.
evidence.source_reference: G. A. Miller, 'The Magical Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two,' Psychological Review (1956)

evidence.description: Telephone number formatting (803-296-2632) as a demonstration of memory chunking.
Sentence recall examples ('The cat is on the mat') demonstrating how phrasal units allow for the recall of 16+ letters.
T.G. Bever's three-step model of perceptual processing (clause as primary unit, semantic assignment, recoding into gist).
evidence.source_reference: T. G. Bever, 'Perception and Thought in Language' (1972)

evidence.description: Jacqueline Sachs's 1967 study showing subjects could not distinguish between original sentences and those with changed syntax (passive/active) shortly after hearing them, but could distinguish meaning changes.
evidence.source_reference: J. S. Sachs, 'Recognition Memory for Syntactic and Semantic Aspects of Connected Discourse' (1967)
evidence.source_passage: She read her subjects narratives that were one paragraph long. Each paragraph contained a target sentence... Sachs inferred from these results, and later experimentation has [confirmed that surface form is lost].

evidence.description: Subjects shown a sentence about Galileo were unable to distinguish between the original and a passive-voice version shortly after hearing it, but could distinguish it from a sentence with changed meaning.
evidence.source_reference: Jacqueline Sachs (1967)
evidence.source_passage: A typical original sentence was “He sent a letter about it to Galileo, the great Italian scientist.” The two test sentences for this original were “A letter about it was sent to Galileo, the great Italian scientist” and “Galileo, the great Italian scientist, sent him a letter about it.”

evidence.description: Subjects frequently misidentified 'The window is open' as the original sentence when the original was actually 'The window is not closed,' showing a preservation of gist over literal form.
evidence.source_reference: S. Fillenbaum (1966)
evidence.source_passage: Subjects selected (d) [The window is open] far more often than any other incorrect choice, showing that they had indeed preserved an accurate memory for meaning.

evidence.description: A series of recognition tests using sentences about turtles and logs showed that subjects mistakenly believe inferred information (that a fish swam under a log) was explicitly stated in the text.
evidence.source_reference: J. R. Barclay, J. D. Bransford, and J. J. Franks (1972)
evidence.source_passage: Subjects who originally heard the sentence that said the turtles rested on a log would think the original sentence explicitly said that a fish swam under the log, even though the sentence didn’t mention how the fish and the log related to each other.

evidence.description: The Bransford, Barclay, and Franks experiment regarding turtles on/beside a log showed that subjects mistakenly recall explicit words that fit the mental model they constructed, proving the constructive hypothesis.
evidence.source_reference: Bransford, Barclay and Franks (1972)

evidence.description: Sachs demonstrated that humans have poor memory for specific words but high memory for the general meaning of sentences.
evidence.source_reference: Sachs (1967)

evidence.description: An experiment using a vague passage about 'Washing Clothes' showed that only subjects provided with the title before reading could understand or recognize the sentences.
evidence.source_reference: Bransford and Johnson (1972)

evidence.description: Research on five- and eight-year-olds found that knowledge, not cognitive mechanics like memory or eye movement, accounted for reading differences.
evidence.source_reference: Omanson, Warren, and Trabasso (1978)

evidence.description: A study of second graders with varying knowledge of spiders showed that domain knowledge predicted comprehension better than general IQ or reading scores.
evidence.source_reference: Pearson, Hanson, and Gordon (1979)

evidence.description: A study showing that reading differences between 5 and 8-year-olds are based on knowledge rather than cognitive capacity.
evidence.source_reference: R. C. Omanson, W. H. Warren, and T. Trabasso (1978)

evidence.description: Experiment testing second graders on spider knowledge; found those with prior knowledge read about spiders better than peers with equal IQ but no knowledge.
evidence.source_reference: P. D. Pearson, J. Hanson, C. Gordon (1979)

evidence.description: Language acquisition research showing children learn 'tree' before 'oak' and 'dog' before 'animal'.
evidence.source_reference: Rosch, Mervis, Gray, Johnson, and Boyes-Braem (1976)

evidence.description: Ranking experiments where subjects showed high agreement on typicality of items (e.g., apple as most typical fruit; chair as most typical furniture).
evidence.source_reference: Eleanor Rosch (1975)

evidence.description: Rosch's typicality rankings for categories like fruit, furniture, and birds, showing clear cultural consensus on 'typical' members.
evidence.source_reference: Rosch, E. (1975). Cognitive Representation of Semantic Categories.
evidence.source_passage: Under “furniture” Rosch found the following ranking chair lamp dresser cupboard davenport radio footstool ashtray

evidence.description: Rosch's sentence substitution experiment where subjects judged the 'sense' of sentences when class names were replaced with typical vs. atypical members.
evidence.source_reference: Rosch, E. (1973). On the Internal Structure of Perceptual and Semantic Categories.
evidence.source_passage: She found that if you replace bird with robin in each of the above sentences, subjects always say that the sentences make sense, but if you replace bird with ostrich or chicken, subjects say that the sentences don’t make sense.

evidence.description: Reaction time data showing subjects take longer to verify atypical members of a class (e.g., 'A chicken is a bird') than typical members.
evidence.source_reference: Rosch, E. (1973). On the Internal Structure of Perceptual and Semantic Categories.
evidence.source_passage: For instance, it takes a subject much longer to say true or false to the sentence “A chicken is a bird” than to “A robin is a bird.”

evidence.description: The context-sensitivity of words like 'cup', 'eat', 'red', and 'held' in different linguistic environments.
evidence.source_reference: Labov (1973); Anderson & Ortony (1975); Halff, Ortony & Anderson (1976).
evidence.source_passage: Demonstrations of this variability have been conducted with the words cup, eat, red, and held.

evidence.description: Anderson's three-sentence comparison regarding 'kicking a ball,' showing how different subjects (punter, baby, golfer) imply different objects.
evidence.source_reference: Anderson, R. C., & Shifrin, Z. (1980). The Meaning of Words in Context.
evidence.source_passage: 1. The punter kicked the ball. 2. The baby kicked the ball. 3. The golfer kicked the ball. A different sort of ball is implied by each sentence.

evidence.description: Analysis of three sentences ('The punter/baby/golfer kicked the ball') demonstrating how word meanings like 'kick' and 'ball' change based on the agent's identity.
The 'Thanksgiving feast' example showing how contextual frames dictate specific bird types (turkey over robin).
Johnson-Laird's cognitive modeling of schemata into static pictures and scripts/procedures.
Retrieval time research regarding semantic memory.
evidence.source_reference: A. M. Collins and M. R. Quillian, 'Retrieval Time from Semantic Memory', 1969

evidence.description: The 'Canary' schema example, illustrating that cultural associations (small, yellow, sings) are more functional for communication than biological facts (has DNA, has a backbone).
evidence.source_reference: Collins and Quillian (1969)

evidence.description: Artificial Intelligence (AI) research showing that skill execution is dependent on specific, detailed mental schemata.
evidence.source_reference: General AI research consensus cited by Hirsch

evidence.description: Data showing community college students have high skill in reading about friendship but low skill in reading about the Civil War (Grant and Lee).
evidence.source_reference: Previously cited community college study in Chapter 7

evidence.description: Adriaan de Groot's chess experiments showing grand masters have 100% recall for real game positions but novice-level recall for random positions.
evidence.source_reference: Adriaan de Groot, Het Denken van den Schaker (1946)

evidence.description: Replication of de Groot's findings in fields including algebra, physics, and medicine.
The functional success of AI chess programs that imitate expert knowledge-based procedures rather than brute-force calculation.
De Groot's chess experiments exposing subjects to boards for six seconds, showing novices only remember ~6 pieces.
evidence.source_reference: A. de Groot, Het Denken van den Schaker, 1946 / Thought and Choice in Chess, 1965
evidence.source_passage: When de Groot’s subjects were exposed to a chessboard for about six seconds, the limits of short-term memory were quickly reached... novices could manage to place only about six pieces

evidence.description: De Groot's second experiment showing experts perform like novices when pieces are in random patterns.
evidence.source_reference: A. de Groot, Thought and Choice in Chess, 1965
evidence.source_passage: Like everyone else, they can remember only five or six pieces from a random pattern.

evidence.description: Estimates that chess masters recognize ~50,000 patterns, roughly the same size as a literate person's vocabulary.
evidence.source_reference: Larkin, et al., 'Expert and Novice Performance in Solving Physics Problems'
evidence.source_passage: It has been estimated that a chess master can recognize about 50,000 positional patterns. Interestingly, that is the approximate number of words and idioms in the vocabulary of a literate person.

evidence.description: The example of mathematical symbols representing decades of labor.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch (authorial example)
evidence.source_passage: many decades of mathematical labor can be reduced to a single symbol that can be manipulated as though it were a mere digit like the number five.

evidence.description: The symbolic nature of mathematics where a single symbol represents decades of labor and complex associated meanings.
Experimental data showing that children (K-9th grade) perform poorly on a shape-communication task when a physical barrier prevents visual cues, whereas adults succeed instantly.
evidence.source_reference: R. M. Krauss and S. Glucksberg, 'Social and Nonsocial Speech,' Scientific American 236 (1977)
evidence.source_passage: The older children did show marked improvement with practice, but it seemed clear that even the fifth- graders (who were about 10 years old) did not approach the adult level.

evidence.description: The Krauss-Glucksberg experiment involving children and adults describing strange shapes across a physical barrier to test social vs. nonsocial speech.
evidence.source_reference: R. M. Krauss and S. Glucksberg, “Social and Nonsocial Speech,” Scientific American 236 (February 1977): 100-105.
evidence.source_passage: As the Krauss-Glucksberg experiments indicate, literate adults have a surer instinct for what will and will not be shared by others in the wider culture.

evidence.description: Basil Bernstein's experiments showing that child performance on verbal tasks varies with literacy and social class, depending on exposure to 'literate strangers.'
evidence.source_reference: B. Bernstein, “Social Class, Language and Socialization,” in Language and Social Context, ed. P. P. Giglioli (1972).
evidence.source_passage: Experiments reported by Basil Bernstein suggest that the performances of children attempting tasks similar to those in the Krauss Glucksberg experiments vary according to the children’s degree of literacy.

evidence.description: A 2021 critical review confirming the essential role of background knowledge in reading comprehension across decades of research.
evidence.source_reference: Reid Smith et al., 'The Role of Background Knowledge in Reading Comprehension: A Critical Review,' Reading Psychology (2021).

concepts.author_definition: A fictional category of transferable skills (like 'finding the main idea') that schools claim to teach but which do not actually exist independently of background knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: The widely held educational belief that reading is a set of formal skills that can be applied to any text regardless of content.
concepts.source_passage: Current science explains why the claim made by our schools that they are teaching general “reading-comprehension skills” is a delusion.

concepts.author_definition: A deficit in the 'national-ethnic background knowledge' shared by the literate members of a society, rather than a lack of cognitive skill.
A cognitive process of grouping discrete items into smaller numbers of meaningful groupings to bypass the hardwired limits of short-term memory.
concepts.source_passage: We can remember telephone numbers and other series of discrete items if we chunk the disparate items into a smaller number of groupings.

concepts.author_definition: A metaphor describing the structure of written text, where explicit words are only the tip and the vast majority of meaning lies in the reader's background knowledge.
concepts.source_passage: The explicit meanings of a piece of writing are the tip of an iceberg of meaning; the larger part lies below the surface of the text and is composed of the reader’s own relevant knowledge

concepts.author_definition: The 'vestibule' of the mind; a temporary suspension state where incoming items are briefly held to be structured into clauses before being recoded for long-term storage.
The shorthand, abstract recoding of meaning that is transferred to long-term memory, as opposed to the literal surface form or specific words.
The precise words and syntactic structure of a sentence.
The theory that language understanding involves constructing an elaborate mental model of what words imply, incorporating background knowledge, and storing that model in long-term memory.
concepts.common_alternative: A theory that we store the literal meanings of clauses and sentences directly in long-term memory.

concepts.author_definition: An integrated cognitive structure formed by combining words from a text with relevant prior knowledge to achieve discourse understanding.
Individuals who lack the cultural background knowledge necessary to recognize referential clues in standard texts, leading to a breakdown in comprehension.
Classifications that are neither overly specific nor overly general, which children learn first and adults use as default cognitive tools.
concepts.common_alternative: In formal logic, any level of a hierarchy is equally valid; Hirsch/Rosch argue for psychological primacy of the middle.
concepts.source_passage: Eleanor Rosch has called these middle categories basic-level terms... she has argued that middle-level categories are the basic furniture of our conceptual world.

concepts.author_definition: The degree to which a specific example (e.g., robin) is seen as representative of a general category (e.g., bird) within a specific culture.
concepts.source_passage: people understand these normal classifications through commonly encountered examples.

concepts.author_definition: The internal framework that allows the mind to bridge the gap between fragmented sensory input and meaningful wholes using past experience.
concepts.source_passage: The raw data of visual or linguistic perception come in bits and pieces, and they will remain meaningless if they continue to be nothing but colored flecks or mere sequences of letters or words. In our minds these transitory fragments can acquire meaning only by being placed within larger, not presently visible wholes that are based on past knowledge.

concepts.author_definition: A typical exemplar of a category word held in the mind that represents the 'usual' instance of that category in a specific culture.
Abstract mental entities or models (also called frames, scripts, or theories) used to assimilate present experiences to past knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: Sometimes confused with concrete mental images, which Hirsch rejects.
concepts.source_passage: Schema and its plural schemata correctly suggest somewhat abstract mental entities rather than concrete images.

concepts.author_definition: Hilary Putnam's term for the mental models or prototypes that enable comprehension within a linguistic community.
concepts.source_passage: These mental models are the same as Hilary Putnam’s “stereotypes.”

concepts.author_definition: The structural limitation of short-term memory that forces readers to process information in small, integrated chunks.
The reciprocal interaction where past background knowledge (schemata) interprets incoming words, while those words simultaneously refine or adjust the chosen schemata.
The ability of a single element of a schema to represent an entire complex of subterranean elements, reducing cognitive load.

The distinctive system of traits and implicit information commonly associated with a concept within a specific culture (e.g., U.S. Grant being a general and a drinker) that are retrieved in milliseconds during reading.
The view that reading is not a single, generalizable 'skill' but a collection of domain-specific abilities dependent on the reader's background knowledge of the subject matter.
The part of a mental knowledge structure containing the most frequently used and most directly available information.
The 'intellectual muscle' theory of education which posits that critical thinking or reading can be developed as all-purpose abilities.
concepts.source_passage: a skill is not a unified system of intellectual muscles that can be developed by calisthenics into a vigorous all-purpose ability.

concepts.author_definition: The optimal level at which indexed mental schemata should be stored to maximize cognitive effectiveness and retrieval.
The shared system of associations, words, idioms, and grammatical conventions that belong to the national language community as a whole, enabling communication between strangers.
concepts.source_passage: Words, idioms, and grammatical systems represent shared systems of association102 – common to an ethnicity.

concepts.author_definition: Internalized mental structures of information that are known to be common among the wider culture, allowing for rapid and effective communication.
The distinction between the specific knowledge shared by the broad, literate public (national) and the restricted knowledge shared within a specific family or social class (local).
concepts.source_passage: Bernstein’s experiments reconfirmed that literacy is a function of a national rather than a local culture.

concepts.author_definition: The distinctive system of traits commonly associated with a word in a specific culture that differentiate it from other members of its class (e.g., smallness and yellowness for a canary).
The specific types of mental models—one concerning how to do things and the other concerning what things are—that underly all cognitive skills.
The incoming sequence of words that short-term memory must briefly hold and convert into stable, nontemporal structures.
A satirical term used by Herbert Simon to mock the circular reasoning of attributing expert performance to an innate 'skill' rather than specific knowledge.
Speech that is effectively communicated to others because the speaker understands and accounts for the listener's perspective and shared knowledge.

cases.name: The Baseball Study
cases.description: A study by Recht and Leslie (1988) tested children on their comprehension of a baseball passage. It compared 'good readers' and 'poor readers' as determined by standardized tests, further dividing them by their knowledge of baseball. The study found that poor readers with high baseball knowledge performed as well as or better than good readers with low baseball knowledge.
key_passages: The children who knew a lot about baseball, but were technically poor readers, read the baseball passage a lot better than the good readers who happened not to know very much about baseball.
Students with high reading ability and high knowledge did not recall better or summarize better than did students with low reading ability and high knowledge.
contestable_aspects: The definition of 'reading ability' used in the pre-test
Generalizability of baseball knowledge to other domains

cases.name: The Turtles on a Log Experiment
cases.description: Researchers tested subjects with two variations of a sentence about turtles on or beside a log. One version allowed for an inference about a fish swimming under the log, while the other did not. Subjects who heard the 'on' version later 'remembered' the explicit sentence as including the inference, proving that background knowledge of physical objects (logs, swimming) is automatically integrated into the mental representation of a text.
key_passages: In sentence 2, we can infer from our knowledge of logs, fish, and swimming that the fish did swim under the log.

Subjects who originally heard the sentence that said the turtles rested on a log would think the original sentence explicitly said that a fish swam under the log, even though the sentence didn’t mention how the fish and the log related to each other.

cases.name: The Washing Clothes Experiment
cases.description: Subjects were read a deliberately vague paragraph. Those not given the title 'Washing Clothes' beforehand could not make sense of the passage or remember it, whereas those given the title could use their prior knowledge of laundry to construct a mental model of the text.
key_passages: Some subjects were given the title “Washing Clothes” before they read the passage, some were given it after, and some were not given it at all. Only the group who had been given the title before they started to read could recognize sentences from the passage.

cases.name: The Spider Knowledge Test
cases.description: Second-grade students with similar reading levels and IQs were tested on their knowledge of spiders before reading a story about spiders. Students with prior spider knowledge performed significantly better than those without it, even when the text lacked difficult vocabulary.
key_passages: Those who knew a little about them were much better readers of a story involving spiders than were the other children, even though the story contained no special concepts or difficult words.

cases.name: Australian Bird Schema
cases.description: In Australia, the mental prototype for 'bird' would likely be large, colorful, and scarlet, green, or blue, modeled after birds like the parakeet or galah commonly seen in gardens, rather than the drab American robin.
key_passages: If Rosch had tested her subjects in Australia... she would have found the typical bird schema to be unlike a robin... The creature nearest the Australian schema would be large and colorful, like a parakeet or a galah.
contestable_aspects: The assumption that all Australians share a single uniform 'bird schema' despite the country's diverse ecosystems.

cases.name: The Canary Schema
cases.description: A cognitive model used to demonstrate how we categorize information. Hirsch uses it to show that functional literacy ignores scientific facts (like canaries having DNA) in favor of cultural markers (canaries being yellow and kept in cages).
key_passages: The schema canary can yield an indefinite number of facts... But this secondary information about canaries is not important in communicating with fellow human beings.

cases.name: U.S. Grant and Appomattox
cases.description: Specific historical examples used to illustrate the 'primary associations' required to understand adult-level text about the American Civil War without stopping to look up basic facts.
key_passages: When we encounter U. S. Grant, the primary associations must be available to us in milliseconds: that he was an important Union general, that he became president, that he drank.

cases.name: De Groot Chess Experiment
cases.description: Dutch psychologist Adriaan de Groot tested chess players of varying ranks on their ability to memorize board positions. When positions were taken from real games, masters and grand masters showed near-perfect recall (90-100%). However, when pieces were placed randomly on the board, the masters performed no better than novices, demonstrating that their 'skill' was actually a function of recognizing known patterns (knowledge) rather than a superior general memory.
key_passages: In one experiment, de Groot displayed for five to ten seconds a chess position from an actual game... Grand masters performed this feat with 100 percent accuracy... Weaker players were lucky if they could correctly place five or six pieces.
Instead of placing the twenty-five pieces in positions from an actual game, he placed them on the board randomly. The results were unexpected. All his subjects... performed the same as novices did

cases.name: Krauss-Glucksberg Communication Experiment
cases.description: An experiment where two subjects are separated by a barrier. A speaker must describe six unfamiliar shapes to a listener. Literate adults succeed immediately. Children (K-9) fail or struggle, using idiosyncratic rather than social associations.
key_passages: The speaker must explain to the listener the order in which the six following unfamiliar shapes must be listed.
Their messages depend upon associations that are not widely shared in the speech community.
Even the fifth-graders... did not approach the adult level.
contestable_aspects: Whether the failure is due to 'information' or simply developmental stages in perspective-taking (theory of mind).

thinkers.name: George Miller
thinkers.key_idea: The human mind has a severely limited capacity for short-term memory, restricted to approximately seven (plus or minus two) discrete items.
thinkers.source_work: The Magical Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two (1956)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Miller's work as the foundational psychological insight to explain why background knowledge (which allows chunking) is necessary for reading comprehension.
key_passages: Miller noticed, though, that our capacity for remembering briefly presented and disconnected items is severely limited. The mind cannot reliably hold more than about four to seven separate items in short term memory

thinkers.name: Robert Glaser
thinkers.key_idea: Decades of research have created an unparalleled understanding of the underlying processes of language comprehension.
thinkers.source_work: Foreword to 'Becoming a Nation of Readers' (1985)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Glaser to establish the scientific consensus that reading is an active, knowledge-based process rather than a simple sequential identification of words.
key_passages: What so impressed Glaser and his distinguished co-panelists on the National Commission on Reading? For one thing, new research refuted the old thinking that reading follows an orderly pattern.

thinkers.name: T. G. Bever
thinkers.key_idea: The clause is the primary perceptual unit of language; it is processed, assigned semantic relations, and then recoded into abstract form.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Bever's three-step model to explain how the mind manages to remember meanings despite the fleeting nature of short-term memory.
key_passages: As the psycholinguist T. G. Bever explains in three steps : “(1) The clause is the primary perceptual unit; (2) within each clause we assign semantic relations within major phrases; (3) after each clause is processed, it is recoded into relatively abstract form”

thinkers.name: Jacqueline Sachs
thinkers.key_idea: Surface forms of sentences (syntax and specific wording) are lost to memory almost immediately, while semantic content is retained.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Sachs's 'Galileo' experiment to provide empirical proof that literal word memory is poor compared to gist memory.
key_passages: In 1967 Jacqueline Sachs reported a demonstration of this phenomenon. She read her subjects narratives that were one paragraph long. Each paragraph contained a target sentence.

thinkers.name: G. A. Miller
thinkers.key_idea: The limit of human short-term memory is approximately seven items (plus or minus two).
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch references Miller's foundational research (Endnote 72) regarding the 'magical number seven' as a hardwired constraint.
key_passages: ly remember more than four to seven discrete items that have been presented once without rehearsal.

thinkers.name: J. R. Barclay, J. D. Bransford, and J. J. Franks
thinkers.key_idea: The constructive nature of language understanding.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch highlights their work as the start of a 'next phase' of research that shifted focus to how background knowledge is applied during initial understanding.
key_passages: The next phase of work started in 1972 with an important paper by J. R. Barclay, J. D. Bransford, and J. J. Franks.

thinkers.name: R. J. Spiro
thinkers.key_idea: The discovery that inferential elaborations are essential to understanding prose led to an 'avalanche' of confirming studies.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Spiro's quote to characterize the overwhelming scientific consensus regarding the constructive nature of reading.
key_passages: According to R. J. Spiro, “The study by Bransford, Barclay and Franks served as a prototype for what was to become a near avalanche of demonstrations that inferential elaborations are a part of the process of understanding prose.”

thinkers.name: P. D. Pearson
thinkers.key_idea: Domain-specific knowledge is a more accurate predictor of reading performance than general aptitude tests for young children.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Pearson's spider study to show that background knowledge is the 'active ingredient' in reading skill, independent of IQ.
key_passages: Similar work by P. D. Pearson and his associates has shown that, among seven-year-olds who score the same on reading and IQ tests, those who have greater knowledge relevant to the text at hand show superior reading skills.

thinkers.name: T. Trabasso
